name: Dinosaur Footprint Reservation, New Bed
short_description: Explore Triassic-age sedimentary beds in the Connecticut River Valley near Easthampton. Hunt for minerals, fossils, and geological specimens in this historic fossiliferous site.
description:
The Dinosaur Footprint Reservation's New Bed is located in one of North America's most important Mesozoic fossil localities, situated within the Connecticut River Valley of Massachusetts. This site preserves Early Jurassic to Late Triassic sedimentary rocks that were deposited in an ancient rift basin over 200 million years ago. The New Bed specifically exposes fine-grained sandstones, siltstones, and mudstones that contain impressions, trace fossils, and occasionally preserved mineral specimens.

The geology here reflects a time when this region was an active basin with seasonal flooding and river deposition. Quartz, feldspar, and mica are the primary mineral components of the rocks exposed here, making mineral-focused rockhounders will find abundant small crystals and aggregates within the matrix. The flattened terrain at 184 feet elevation makes access relatively straightforward, though the sedimentary nature of the beds requires careful, respectful collection to preserve scientific value.

Visitors should expect to find small mineral specimens, fossil imprints of trace fossils (burrows, tracks), and occasional rounded quartz pebbles within the matrix. The site has educational value for rockhounders interested in stratigraphy, mineralogy, and paleontology. Local geologic museums and university collections have referenced specimens from this location, making it a scientifically significant rockhounding destination.

The reservation setting means access is managed for preservation, so follow all posted guidelines. Early morning visits during dry conditions provide the best footing and specimen visibility. Bring a field guide to Connecticut Valley geology to enhance your understanding of the sedimentary layers you will encounter.
history: The Connecticut River Valley Triassic rocks, including those at this reservation, were deposited approximately 230-200 million years ago during active rifting and crustal extension. This ancient rift basin received sediment from surrounding highlands, creating alternating layers of sandstone and mudstone that today preserve one of the world's best records of Early Mesozoic vertebrate and invertebrate life. The reservation was established to protect these scientifically invaluable deposits from unrestricted quarrying.
tips: Focus your search on exposed faces of the New Bed where weathering has loosened mineral specimens and trace fossils. Use a hand lens to identify small mica flakes and quartz crystals within the sandstone matrix, and look for darker seams where iron oxide concentration may concentrate mineral deposits. Bring a geology hammer and chisel for careful extraction, and avoid heavy prying that damages the irreplaceable sedimentary layers. The flat terrain means you can spend more time searching; systematically walk the exposed outcrop edges rather than concentrating on one spot.
terrain_notes: Elevation 184 feet with flat topography typical of valley floor terrain. Walking surface consists of exposed sedimentary bedrock and loose shale fragments. Vegetation is sparse to moderate depending on season, with some brushy growth on less-worked areas. Footing is generally stable on bedrock but can be slippery when wet. Accessible parking to outcrop distance typically under 0.5 miles with uneven ground.
best_season: April - October. Spring and early summer offer dry conditions and good specimen visibility. Avoid November - March when freeze-thaw cycles destabilize loose rock and wet conditions make footing hazardous.
written_directions: From Easthampton center, take Route 10 north approximately 1.5 miles. The Dinosaur Footprint Reservation entrance is on the east side of Route 10 near the intersection with Mountain Road. Follow posted signs for parking and New Bed access point. Parking is a small designated area; do not block access roads.
road_conditions: Paved road to parking area. Parking access via maintained gravel/dirt drive. No high-clearance vehicle needed.
parking_notes: Small designated parking area at reservation entrance. Limited capacity, typically 10-15 vehicles. No facilities; arrive early during peak season. Respect all posted parking restrictions.
collection_rules: This is a state-protected reservation. Collection of specimens is permitted for personal use in small quantities only. Do not remove large blocks or sections of bedrock. Respect all posted signs and boundaries. No commercial collection or sale of specimens. Preservation of the site for scientific research and future generations is paramount. Check with Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation for current access policies.
quantity_limits: Personal use only; keep collection to hand-portable specimens. No large-scale excavation or extraction.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection or sale
No motorized tools or heavy excavation
Respect all posted boundaries and closures
Leave the site as you found it
Protect trace fossils and layered rock structures
Check current DCR regulations before visiting
nearest_services: Easthampton, approximately 1.5 miles south. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with stable bedrock walking surface makes this accessible to most hikers and families. Uneven rock fragments and occasional loose shale require sturdy footwear and careful footing. No wheelchair access to the New Bed outcrop itself, though reservation parking area is accessible. Limited mobility visitors may find the short walk manageable but should assess terrain before committing to the site.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Dinosaur Footprint Reservation in Massachusetts?
faq.answer: The New Bed exposes quartz, feldspar, and mica as primary mineral constituents. Small quartz crystals and mica flakes are the most commonly found specimens. You may also encounter trace fossils, fossil impressions, and rounded quartz pebbles within the sandstone and siltstone matrix. Use a hand lens to spot small crystalline material within the rock faces.

faq.question: Is rockhounding allowed at the Dinosaur Footprint Reservation near Easthampton?
faq.answer: Yes, personal use collection is permitted at this state reservation in small quantities. Commercial collection is strictly prohibited. You must respect all posted boundaries and preserve the site for scientific value. Always check with the Massachusetts Department of Conservation and Recreation for current access and collection policies before your visit.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at the New Bed in Dinosaur Footprint Reservation?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at this reservation. However, you should familiarize yourself with all posted regulations and guidelines. The site is managed to protect its scientific and paleontological importance, so follow all instructions regarding collection methods and quantity limits.

faq.question: What is the best season to visit Dinosaur Footprint Reservation for rockhounding?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best rockhounding conditions at the New Bed. Spring and early summer provide dry conditions and optimal specimen visibility. Avoid winter months (November - March) when freeze-thaw cycles destabilize loose rock and wet conditions create slippery footing on the sedimentary layers.

faq.question: What tools and preparation do I need for rockhounding at the Dinosaur Footprint Reservation?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, chisel, and hand lens for identifying small minerals and fossils. Wear sturdy hiking boots for stable footing on uneven rock surfaces. Pack a field guide to Connecticut Valley Triassic geology to understand the sedimentary layers. A small backpack for specimens, water, and sun protection is essential. Avoid heavy prying tools that damage the irreplaceable rock formations.

faq.question: Is the Dinosaur Footprint Reservation New Bed suitable for beginner rockhounders and families?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent site for beginners and families. The flat terrain and short walking distance from parking make it accessible. Children age 6+ can participate, and the variety of minerals and fossils provides educational value. The relatively safe environment and protection of a managed reservation make it ideal for teaching rockhounding ethics and geology fundamentals.

meta_description: Hunt quartz, feldspar, and mica at Dinosaur Footprint Reservation New Bed near Easthampton, MA. Public state site with trace fossils. No permit needed.
